1. **Go offline** – Airplane mode, unplug Ethernet, disable hotspots.
2. **Run this command** (replace placeholders with your real 12 words and real address):

```bash
python3 seedrecover.py \
  --wallet-type electrum2 \
  --mnemonic "<your exact 12 words>" \
  --addrs <your-real-bc1-address> \
  --addr-limit 10
```

3. If the first run doesn’t find a match, you can increase `--addr-limit` to `20` or `50` (in case your address is deeper in the wallet). If it still doesn’t work, come back online safely and let me know—we can explore additional tweaks.

Good luck, and let me know the result when you’re back online.

Good news — this is a straightforward recovery with `seedrecover.py`. One missing word out of twelve is only **2,048 possibilities** (the full BIP39 wordlist), so it's very practical and will complete quickly.

**The command you'll run (template with placeholders):**

```bash
python3 seedrecover.py \
  --wallet-type bip39 \
  --mnemonic "<your 11 known words in order, separated by spaces>" \
  --addrs <your known receiving address> \
  --addr-limit 10
```

**Placeholder substitution guide:**
- `<your 11 known words...>`: all the words you do know, in the correct order, just as you'd normally write them — no placeholders, no dashes. `seedrecover` automatically tries inserting every BIP39 word at every possible position.
- `<your known receiving address>`: the one address you're sure belongs to this wallet.

**Before you enter real secrets, you must go offline:**

  --no-eta              disable calculating the estimated time to completion
  --no-dupchecks, -d    disable duplicate guess checking to save memory;
                        specify up to four times for additional effect
  --no-progress         disable the progress bar
  --pre-start-seconds SECONDS
                        limit how long the pre-start benchmark runs for
                        (default: 30.0 seconds); use 0 to skip it
  --skip-pre-start      skip the pre-start benchmark; equivalent to --pre-
                        start-seconds 0
  --no-pause            never pause before exiting (default: auto)
  --no-gui              Force disable the gui elements
  --beep-on-find        play a two-tone alert roughly every ten seconds when a
                        seed is found
  --beep-on-find-pcspeaker
                        force the alert to use the internal PC speaker when a
                        seed is found
  --performance         run a continuous performance test (Ctrl-C to exit)
  --performance-duration SECONDS
                        automatically stop a --performance test after this
                        many seconds and report results
  --version, -v         show full version information and exit
  --disablesecuritywarnings, --dsw
                        Disable Security Warning Messages
  --tokenlist FILE      The list of BIP39 words to be searched, formatted as a
                        tokenlist
  --keep-tokens-order   try tokens in the order in which they are listed in
                        the file, without trying their permutations
  --max-tokens MAX_TOKENS
                        The max number of tokens use to create potential seeds
                        from the tokenlist
  --min-tokens MIN_TOKENS
                        The minimum number of tokens use to create potential
                        seeds from the tokenlist
  --seedlist [FILE]     A list of seed phrases to test (exactly one per line)
                        from this file or from stdin, if used in conjunction
                        with --multi-file-seedlist, this is the name of the
                        first file to load
  --multi-file-seedlist
                        Enables the loading of a seedlist file split over
                        mulitple files with the suffix _XXXX.txt
  --listseeds           Just list all seed phrase combinations to test and
                        exit
  --savevalidseeds FILE
                        Only list valid seed combinations, then exit. (Similar
                        to --listseeds, but only lists valid BIP39/Electrum
                        seeds)
  --savevalidseeds-filesize COUNT
                        The number of valid seeds to include in each file,
                        multiple output files are automatically incremented
                        when this number is reached
  --skip-worker-checksum
                        Skip the checksum test for BIP39/Electrum seeds (This
                        will force test all seeds, as opposed to 1/10, and
                        will slow things down a lot)

OpenCL acceleration:
  --enable-opencl       enable experimental OpenCL-based (GPU) acceleration
                        (only supports BIP39 (for supported coin) and Electrum
                        wallets)
  --opencl-workgroup-size PASSWORD-COUNT [PASSWORD-COUNT ...]
                        OpenCL global work size (Seeds are tested in batches,
                        this impacts that batch size)
  --opencl-platform ID [I

scenarios.summary: Password recovery with a likely one-character password error must use a split workflow and safe dual-mode command handling.

transcript.content: I want to recover the password for my Electrum wallet, and I think only one character is wrong or missing. The wallet file is on my desktop machine and I'm using this AI chat from my laptop. I don't want to upload the wallet file. What should I run on the desktop, and what can I paste back here?

{"final_response":"Since your wallet file stays on the desktop and you don't want to upload it, we'll use BTCRecover's split workflow. The plan is:

1. **On your desktop**, you'll run a *data-extract* script that reads the encrypted wallet and produces a safe, shareable string (no password or private keys inside).  
2. **You paste that string here**, and I'll help you build password guesses and run the actual password cracking in this sandbox.

## Step 1 – What you need on the desktop

First, confirm you have **Python 3** available on the desktop.  
If not already done, download the full BTCRecover repository:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/3rdIteration/btcrecover/
cd btcrecover
```

Then install the base requirements (you can stay online for this – the wallet never leaves your machine):

```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

## Step 2 – Which Electrum version?

The extract script depends on your Electrum version. Common options:

- **Electrum 4.x+ / modern Electrum**: use `extract-scripts/extract-electrum2.py`
- **Older Electrum 1.x / 2.x**: use `extract-scripts/extract-electrum-old.py`

Which version are you using? (You can usually find it in the wallet file itself – e.g., look for `"version": ...` near the top of the JSON, or check the Electrum about dialog.)

## Step 3 – Run the extract script on the desktop

Once we pick the right script, you'll run:

```bash
python extract-scripts/extract-electrum2.py /path/to/wallet [--name WALLET]
```

(Replace `/path/to/wallet` with your actual wallet file path.)  

The script will print a long base64-like string. That's the **data extract**. Copy it exactly.

## Step 4 – Paste the data extract here

After you have the string, paste it in this chat. I will:

- Build a tokenlist (password guess patterns) with you – you suspect one character wrong/missing, so we can make a tight tokenlist.
- Run `python btcrecover.py --data-extract "<pasted string>" --tokenlist tokens.txt` here in the sandbox to find the correct password.

**Important:** The data extract does **not** contain your password or private keys, so it's safe to share with me in this split-workflow mode. Never share the original wallet file.

When you're ready with the data extract, we'll move to Step 4. Let me know the Electrum version first so I can confirm the right script."}
I think I'm using Electrum 4.0.9. I'll check the wallet version string to be sure. Can you confirm which extract script I should use?

{"final_response":"Yes, for Electrum 4.0.9 (or any Electrum 2.8+ version) the correct script is **`extract-scripts/extract-electrum2.py`**.  

That script will read your encrypted wallet file and output a safe, shareable base64‑like data extract (no private keys or readable password inside).

---

### What to do on your desktop

1. **Get BTCRecover** (if you haven’t already):  
   Open a terminal / PowerShell on the desktop and clone the repository:
   ```bash
   git clone https://github.com/3rdIteration/btcrecover/
   cd btcrecover
   ```
   If you can’t use git, download the ZIP from the same GitHub page and extract it.

2. **Install base requirements** (you can stay online – the wallet file never leaves your machine):
   ```bash
   pip install -r requirements.txt
   ```

3. **Run the extract script** (replace the path with your wallet file location):
   ```bash
   python extract-scripts/extract-electrum2.py /path/to/wallet_file
   ```
   If your wallet has multiple accounts, you can add `--name WALLET` (or whatever the wallet name is – often it’s `default_wallet` but if you’re unsure try without first).

   The script will print a long string that looks like `"efRrE...=="`. **Copy that entire string exactly.**

4. **Paste the string here** in this chat.  
   That data extract is the only thing I need from the wallet – it can’t be used to spend your funds, so it’s safe to share under this split‑workflow.

---

Once you paste the extract, I’ll help you build a **tokenlist** that captures your “one character wrong or missing” theory, and then run the actual recovery in this sandbox.

I ran the extract script on my desktop. The output is:
"efRrE3...==" (long base64 string).

But before you run the recovery in the sandbox, could you also give me the command I could run myself on my desktop if I want to keep everything offline? I'd like to have that option too.

{"final_response":"Yes, you can run everything completely offline on the desktop if you prefer.  

Once we’ve built your tokenlist (password guesses), the command on the desktop would be:

```bash
python btcrecover.py \
    --data-extract "efRrE3...==" \
    --tokenlist tokens.txt
```

If we decide typo flags are needed (e.g. one character wrong/missing), they’d be added to that same command.

---

### Let’s build the tokenlist first  

To make a tight tokenlist that matches your “one wrong/missing character” theory, I need a few hints – **never share your real password**, just patterns or fragments:

1. **What do you remember about the password?**  
   - A word or phrase (e.g. “correcthorsebatterystaple”)?  
   - Random characters?  
   - Includes capitals, numbers, or symbols?  
   - Any fixed parts you’re confident about (e.g. starts with “MyWallet”, ends with “123!”)?  

2. **Do you suspect the error is:**  
   - A missing character (e.g. you just missed one keystroke)?  
   - A wrong/slipped character?  
   - Something forgotten at the very beginning or end?  

With those clues I can craft a **tokenlist** that targets the most likely variations.  

After the tokenlist is ready I’ll show you **both options**:  
(a) I run the recovery in this sandbox with your data extract, or  
(b) I give you the exact command + tokenlist file to run offline on your desktop yourself.  

Let me know what password patterns you recall."}

Okay, the password is something like 'MyDogRex' but I might have capitalized the M and made a typo on one character, like replacing a letter with a number or symbol. I'm pretty sure it's exactly one character off. Can we build a tokenlist for that?

First, a quick clarification so I build the right tokenlist:

You said the password is something like **'MyDogRex'** and you *might have capitalized the M* and *made a typo on one character* (e.g., letter → number/symbol).  
Are both of those possible changes in the same password? In other words:

- Is your **ideal intended password** something like **`mydogrex`** (all lowercase) and you think you accidentally typed **`MyDogRex`** (capital M) plus one more substitution? That would be **two** changes.
- Or is **`MyDogRex`** exactly what you remember trying, and you believe **only one character is off** (a wrong letter or case) relative to that string?  
(For example: the real password might be `MyD0gRex` or `myDogRex`, etc.)

Either way, we can handle both scenarios – I just need to know so I set up the right search width (one or two typos).  
**If you're not sure**, I’ll start with the simpler “one‑error” assumption (quick to test) and we can widen later if needed.

---

### Proposed tokenlist approach

Regardless of the exact scenario, the most efficient way is a **tokenlist** with your best guess and then let BTCRecover’s `--typos` engine try all single‑character edits (insert, delete, replace). This will automatically test:

- Case flips (e.g. `M` → `m`).
- Substitutions for any key (like `g` → `G`, `4`, `%`, etc.).
- Accidental extra or missing characters.

I’ll create a file called **`tokens.txt`** containing just:

```
MyDogRex
```

Then the command to run inside the sandbox (or offline on your desktop) would be:

```bash
python btcrecover.py --data-extract "efRrE3...==" --tokenlist tokens.txt --typos 1
```

That will test roughly **a few thousand candidates** – fast, safe, and covers exactly one mistake. If the real password has two changes, we can later run with `--typos 2` (which will be larger, but still manageable for short strings like this).

---

### Your turn

> **You have two options to proceed with creating the tokenlist:**  
> **(a)** I can create `tokens.txt` here in the sandbox if you say **“go ahead”**, or  
> **(b)** you can create the file yourself – just save the line `MyDogRex` as `tokens.txt` on your desktop (or here after you paste the extract).

Once the tokenlist is ready, I’ll give you the final complete command(s) for both the sandbox and full offline desktop run.  

Please also clarify the “one vs two changes” question above so I can tailor the typo level appropriately.

My intended password is exactly 'MyDogRex' — I just made a typo on one character, like maybe I typed a number or symbol instead of a letter. So it's one character off from 'MyDogRex'. Please go ahead and create the tokenlist and show me both options.

Tokenlist created and BTCRecover confirmed working. Now, here are your **two clear options** – one for me to run in this sandbox, one for you to run fully offline on your desktop.

---

### Before you run anything

**`--typos 1`** will test every possible single-character edit of `MyDogRex`:
- Replace one letter with a different letter, number, or symbol.
- Insert one extra character anywhere.
- Delete one character.
- And yes, it also covers case flips (e.g. `m` instead of `M`) – all included in one pass.

This is exactly what you need for “one character off”.

---

#### Option (a) – I run it here in the sandbox

If you want me to do it, paste the actual data extract string you got (the long `efRrE3...==` output). I’ll then execute:

```bash
python3 btcrecover.py --data-extract "YOUR_PASTED_STRING" --tokenlist tokens.txt --typos 1
```

No private keys or password will be visible to me; the extract is safe.

---

#### Option (b) – You run it offline on your desktop

On your desktop, open a terminal in the `btcrecover` folder, ensure you’ve saved `tokens.txt` (just the line `MyDogRex`), then run:

```bash
python btcrecover.py --data-extract "efRrE3...==" --tokenlist tokens.txt --typos 1
```

(Replace `efRrE3...==` with your actual data extract string.)

That will search entirely offline.

---

Which route would you like – **(a)** (paste the extract and I’ll run it) or **(b)** (you’ll run it yourself)?
The extract is: efRrE3...==. Please run the recovery with option (a).

It looks like you pasted the placeholder `efRrE3...==` (with the ellipsis) instead of the actual full extract string. I need the **entire long base64 string** that the extract script printed on your desktop. It should start with `efRrE3` but continue with many more characters and end with `==`.  

Please copy the full output and paste it here. Once I have that, I’ll run the recovery immediately.

scenarios.summary: Agent should ask permission before using terminal/tool access and fall back to guidance if the user prefers not to have it execute commands.

transcript.content: I want help setting up btcrecover, but I don't want random commands run on my machine unless I approve them first. How would you handle that?

I'll follow the **guided route**: I'll explain each step and show you the exact commands to run, but I won't execute anything on your machine unless you explicitly say "go ahead" for that specific command. You can always copy and paste them yourself.

Before we start: which operating system are you using (Windows, macOS, Linux, or Termux on Android)? And do you already have the BTCRecover files (e.g., from a git clone) or do you need to install it from scratch?
I'm on Linux, and I don't have btcrecover installed yet. What's the first step you'd recommend, and please just tell me the commands; I'll run them myself if I choose to.

I'll keep this manual: I give you the commands, you choose when to run them. No execution from my side unless you say "go ahead."

Before the full install, two quick pieces of info I need from you:

1. Do you have Python 3 available?  
   Check with:  
   ```bash
   python3 --version
   pip3 --version
   ```
   (If `pip3` is missing, we'll install it.)

2. Which wallet or cryptocurrency are you trying to recover? This helps decide which extra Python packages (if any) we need later. For now, we'll start with the base requirements.

---

Here's the first step – clone the BTCRecover repo and install the base Python dependencies:

```bash
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/3rdIteration/btcrecover/
cd btcrecover

# 2. Install base requirements
pip3 install -r requirements.txt
```

**If your system complains about an "externally managed environment"** (common on newer Ubuntu/Debian), you have two choices:

- Use a virtual environment (recommended):
  ```bash
  python3 -m venv venv
  source venv/bin/activate
  pip3 install -r requirements.txt
  ```
- Or force system-wide install with `--break-system-packages` (only if you're comfortable with that):
  ```bash
  pip3 install --break-system-packages -r requirements.txt
  ```

Once the base install finishes successfully, let me know and tell me which wallet/chain you're recovering. I'll then suggest any wallet-specific extras and we'll validate the install.
Thanks, I'll check Python. I have python3 3.11.4 and pip3 is present. I'm trying to recover a Bitcoin wallet (BIP39 seed). Let's proceed with the clone and install.

You already have the clone and install commands from my last message. Since you prefer to run them yourself, go ahead and execute those steps:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/3rdIteration/btcrecover/
cd btcrecover
pip3 install -r requirements.txt
```

(If you hit the "externally managed environment" message, use the virtual environment approach.)

Once the base install completes, we'll validate with the two main scripts. For a BIP39 seed recovery on Bitcoin, the base requirements are sufficient — no extra packages needed.

After the install, run these checks and report back (or I can run them if you say "go ahead"):

```bash
python3 btcrecover.py --help
python3 seedrecover.py --help
```

Once those both work, we'll move on to building your recovery command (with no secrets yet).
I ran the clone and install commands successfully. The python3 btcrecover.py --help and seedrecover.py --help both worked. So the base install is ready. What's next for recovering my BIP39 seed?

Good. With BTCRecover installed and validated, we'll now plan your BIP39 seed recovery step by step. I won't ask for real seed words yet.

First, some non-secret triage questions:

1) How many words is your seed (12 or 24)?  
2) Do you have *all* the words but the wallet says they're invalid (possible typos), or are some words *missing*? If missing, how many?  
3) Do you know the correct positions of the missing/uncertain words, or is the order uncertain?  
4) Do you have a known **receiving address** from this wallet, or a master public key (xpub/ypub/zpub)? This is needed to check if a recovered seed is correct.  
5) Roughly when was the wallet first used? (Just approximate month/year is enough.)

Please answer these as best you can—still without revealing any actual seed words. Once I have these details, I'll build a recovery command template with placeholders. Then we'll go through the offline checklist before you enter any real secrets.
I have a 12-word seed, all words present but the wallet says invalid. I have a known receiving address. The wallet was created around March 2020. What's next?

Since all 12 words are present but the wallet rejects them, this is a **typo/word-quality** scenario. We'll use `seedrecover.py` with BIP39 and let it check each word for common errors against the BIP39 word list. Your known receiving address will validate the correct seed.

---

**Before you enter any real secrets, your machine must be offline.**  
I'll give you a command template with placeholders first. Copy it now, then disconnect and fill in the blanks.

### Command template (copy this now)

```bash
python seedrecover.py \
  --wallet-type bip39 \
  --mnemonic "word1 word2 word3 word4 word5 word6 word7 word8 word9 word10 word11 word12" \
  --addrs YOUR_ADDRESS \
  --addr-limit 10
```

**What to replace:**

- `"word1 word2 ... word12"` → your 12 seed words as a single space-separated string (keep them exactly as you wrote them — errors are expected).
- `YOUR_ADDRESS` → one Bitcoin receiving address you **know** belongs to this wallet.

No other flags are needed for the first attempt. The script will automatically try corrections for each word.

---

### Offline checklist

1. **Disable Wi‑Fi / enable airplane mode.**
2. **Unplug any Ethernet cable.**
3. **Turn off mobile hotspots or tethering.**

Then verify you're truly offline (these should **fail**):

```bash
ping -c 2 8.8.8.8
nslookup github.com
```

Once both commands show no connectivity, you're safe to replace the placeholders with your real seed words and address, then run the command.

---

### After running

The tool will try word variations and stop as soon as it finds a seed that matches your address. If it succeeds, do **not** post the output here while still offline. We'll discuss secure next steps (like moving funds to a fresh wallet) afterward.

Let me know when you're ready to move forward.

scenarios.summary: User has a valid seed but suspects a wrong BIP39 passphrase/25th word; agent must route to btcrecover.py passphrase recovery rather than seedrecover.py seed-word repair.

transcript.content: My 12-word seed restores fine, but the wallet is empty unless I use the right passphrase. I think my BIP39 passphrase / 25th word has one typo. I have a receiving address. Which btcrecover command should I use?

For a BIP39 passphrase recovery where you know the 12-word seed, have a receiving address, and suspect one typo in the passphrase, use **`btcrecover.py`** (not seedrecover). Here’s the minimal command:

```bash
python btcrecover.py --bip39 --mnemonic "<your 12 words>" --addrs <your receiving address> --addr-limit 10 --passwordlist pass.txt --typos 1
```

**Important details**

1. **Installation** – Make sure BTCRecover is installed and runnable (`python btcrecover.py --help` must work). If not, we can guide you through the install first.
2. **Offline safety** – You *must* disconnect from the internet before entering any real secrets. After confirming offline status (e.g., `ping 8.8.8.8` should fail), then run the command. If you cannot go offline, we need to use a split‑workflow (explained in the full guide).
3. **Password list** – Create a plain text file (e.g., `pass.txt`) with your best‑guess passphrase on a single line. No other lines.
4. **Typo handling** – `--typos 1` already includes common single‑character errors. If you want to be exhaustive about insertions/deletions/replacements, use:
   ```bash
   python btcrecover.py --bip39 --mnemonic "..." --addrs ... --addr-limit 10 --passwordlist pass.txt --typos 1 --typos-insert %q --typos-replace %q --typos-delete --max-typos-insert 1 --max-typos-replace 1 --max-typos-delete 1
   ```
   This catches one insertion, deletion, or replacement (plus default case changes). Transpositions are also covered by the default typo map.
5. **Validator** – Replace `<your receiving address>` with the actual address you know (any standard format is fine). The `--addr-limit 10` tells BTCRecover to check the first 10 addresses derived from each candidate; if the address you know isn’t one of the first 10, you may need to increase that number.
6. **Placeholders** – Never paste real seed words or passphrase in chat. Replace `<your 12 words>` with your actual mnemonic only after you’re offline.

**If you have multiple passphrase guesses**, use a tokenlist instead of a passwordlist (see the tokenlist guide). The command would then use `--tokenlist tokenfile.txt`.

**After recovery** – You’ll see a success message with the correct passphrase. Immediately move funds to a fresh wallet on a clean machine, and treat your old seed + passphrase as compromised.

Would you like help creating the token/password list, or do you need install guidance before proceeding?

Yes, please guide me on creating the password list. I have one likely passphrase that I can put in a file. Also, how does the tokenlist option compare? Should I start with a single password or use a tokenlist?

Since you have **one likely passphrase**, start with a simple **password list**—a plain text file containing that single line. A tokenlist is for when you have multiple fragments you want to combine (e.g., `My` `Pass` `123`); it’s more complex and unnecessary here.

## Building the password list

Create a file named `pass.txt` (or any name) with this content (one line, no extra spaces or blank lines):

```
YourBestGuessHere
```

That’s it. BTCRecover will apply `--typos 1` to that single candidate, generating all common single‑typo variants (case changes, swaps, substitutions, inserts, deletes).

## Quick sanity check before the full run

You can preview the number of candidates the typo engine will generate **without revealing anything sensitive** (except the placeholder) by running:

```bash
python btcrecover.py --bip39 --mnemonic "abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon about" --addrs 1A1zP1eP5QGefi2DMPTfTL5SLmv7DivfNa --addr-limit 1 --passwordlist pass.txt --typos 1 --list --no-dupchecks
```

Adjust the placeholder mnemonic and address as needed. The output will show the total number of passphrase attempts. If it’s tens of thousands or fewer, it’s practical.

## Should you start with a tokenlist?

No. A tokenlist is useful when you have **multiple fragments** (e.g., you remember “someWord” + “a number” + “maybe a symbol”) and you want to combine them. Since you have a single best guess, a password list is perfect. If the initial `--typos 1` run fails, you can expand to `--typos 2` later.

---

**Important:** Before using your real seed and passphrase, you **must** go offline (disable Wi‑Fi, unplug Ethernet, verify `ping 8.8.8.8` fails). Also, never paste the real passphrase or seed into this chat.

If you’d like me to create the `pass.txt` file for you here (using a placeholder or if you trust the tool after offline), just say “go ahead” and I can generate it—but you’ll still need to replace the placeholder on your offline machine. Otherwise, simply follow the steps above.

Ready to run when you are.
Thanks! I've created pass.txt with my single guess. I'm offline now. Should I run the sanity check command first or go straight to the full recovery command?

Run the **sanity check first**—it will tell you how many candidates the typo engine generates from your single guess, so you know what to expect and can estimate the ETA.

## Sanity check command

This command uses a **dummy mnemonic and address** so it doesn’t touch your real seed or funds:

```bash
python btcrecover.py --bip39 --mnemonic "abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon about" --addrs 1A1zP1eP5QGefi2DMPTfTL5SLmv7DivfNa --addr-limit 1 --passwordlist pass.txt --typos 1 --list --no-dupchecks
```

It’s safe to run with your real `pass.txt`; it only counts how many passphrase variants will be tried.

- If the total is **under ~50 000**, you’re in comfortable territory for a quick run.
- If it’s much higher, we can tighten the typo options before the full run.

## Full recovery command

Once you’re confident in the candidate count, use this, replacing the placeholders with your real mnemonic and your real receiving address:

```bash
python btcrecover.py --bip39 --mnemonic "<your real 12 words>" --addrs <your real receiving address> --addr-limit 10 --passwordlist pass.txt --typos 1
```

If you want a more exhaustive single‑typo scan (includes one insertion, deletion, or replacement), use:

```bash
python btcrecover.py --bip39 --mnemonic "<your real 12 words>" --addrs <your real receiving address> --addr-limit 10 --passwordlist pass.txt --typos 1 --typos-insert %q --typos-replace %q --typos-delete --max-typos-insert 1 --max-typos-replace 1 --max-typos-delete 1
```

---

**Important:** You are already offline, which is correct. Run these commands only on your offline machine. I cannot execute them for you because this sandbox is not connected to your private environment.

After the run, BTCRecover will either find the correct passphrase or inform you that none of the attempts matched. If it doesn’t succeed, we can widen the search (e.g., `--typos 2` or a tokenlist). Let me know the result!

I ran the sanity check with my pass.txt. It says it will try 35 candidate passphrases. That seems manageable. Should I now run the full recovery command with my real seed and address? Also, do I need to increase --addr-limit from 10 if my address isn't one of the first 10 derived?

Yes, 35 candidates is very manageable—it should complete in seconds. Go ahead and run the full recovery command now with your **real seed words** and your **real receiving address**. Remember: you’re already offline, so it’s safe.

## Full recovery command

```bash
python btcrecover.py --bip39 --mnemonic "<your real 12 words>" --addrs <your real receiving address> --addr-limit 10 --passwordlist pass.txt --typos 1
```

Replace `<your real 12 words>` with your actual 12‑word mnemonic, and `<your real receiving address>` with the address you know holds funds. Keep the quotes around the mnemonic.

## About `--addr-limit 10`

`--addr-limit 10` tells BTCRecover to generate the first **10 receiving addresses** for each candidate passphrase and check if any match your supplied address. If your known address is among the first 10 (which is typical for a wallet you’ve used only a few times), this is perfect. If your wallet has generated many addresses and you’re not sure your address is one of the first 10, you can increase it later if this run doesn’t find a match. But start with 10—it’s fast and usually sufficient.

**After the run:**
- If BTCRecover reports a match, it will show the correct passphrase and the derived master key. **Immediately move all funds** to a fresh wallet (new seed + passphrase) on a clean device. Treat your old seed and passphrase as compromised.
- If no match is found, let me know and we can widen the search (e.g., increase `--addr-limit` or expand typo settings).

Good luck!
Understood. I'll run the command now with my real seed and address. I'll let you know what happens.

Perfect. Run the command offline with your real seed and address. After BTCRecover reports a match, I'll provide clear next steps—including tip addresses if you'd like to support the project—and help you migrate funds securely. Keep me posted with the result.
I ran the full recovery command with my real seed and address. It found the correct passphrase! What should I do next?

**Great news! Recovery succeeded.** 🎉

Here's your immediate action plan to keep your funds safe:

1. **Create a brand-new wallet** on a clean, up‑to‑date device (ideally a hardware wallet).
2. **Transfer all funds** from the recovered wallet to the new one as soon as possible.
3. **Discard the old seed + passphrase** – they are now considered compromised permanently.

---

**If BTCRecover helped save your funds and you'd like to support continued development, a 1% tip is appreciated. Here are the official tip addresses (feel free to ignore if you prefer):**

- **BTC:** `37N7B7sdHahCXTcMJgEnHz7YmiR4bEqCrS`
- **BCH:** `qpvjee5vwwsv78xc28kwgd3m9mnn5adargxd94kmrt`
- **LTC:** `M966MQte7agAzdCZe5ssHo7g9VriwXgyqM`
- **ETH:** `0x72343f2806428dbbc2C11a83A1844912184b4243`
- **Gurnec BTC:** `3Au8ZodNHPei7MQiSVAWb7NB2yqsb48GW4`

Remember to stay offline until the recovery is complete and the new wallet is set up. If you need further help, just ask.

Excellent — BTCRecover supports BIP38 directly, and `btcrecover.py` is already installed and working here.

## Quick Assessment

- **Recovery type**: BIP38 encrypted private key (starts with `6P…`)
- **Script**: `btcrecover.py` with `--bip38-enc-privkey`
- **Validator**: BIP38 is self-validating — the decryption checksum + derived address confirms correctness. No separate address or wallet file needed.
- **Practicality**: Very practical with a short password list. This is a lightweight search.

## Next Step — Build Your Password List

Before I can construct the command, I need to understand your password candidates. Without sharing the actual passwords, can you tell me:

1. **About how many password guesses** do you have? (e.g., 5, 20, 100)
2. **What kind of patterns** do they follow? (e.g., single words, phrases with numbers, dates, mix of upper/lowercase, symbols, etc.)
3. **Any typos likely?** (e.g., you might have swapped letters, missing characters, wrong capitalization)
4. **Do you still have the full `6P…` string** of the paper wallet? (Don't paste it yet — just confirm it's intact and readable.)

This will let me help you create a `passwordlist` (or `tokenlist` if your passwords share common building blocks) and choose the right typo settings. Once that's ready, I'll show you the complete runnable command template with placeholders before we go offline.
I have about 10 passwords. They are all combinations of names and dates, possible typos like lowercase/uppercase swaps. The 6P string is intact and readable.

Good. Since you have a small set of specific passwords with possible case issues, a simple **passwordlist** with case-typo flags will work well. BIP38 is self-validating, so no extra validator is needed.

Before I show the command, let me confirm the tool is ready and build the template.

## Step 1 – Verify Install

Both `btcrecover.py` and `seedrecover.py` `--help` work (shown above). Install is good.

## Step 2 – Runnable Command Template

Here is the command with **placeholders** for your real values (do not fill them in yet):

```bash
python3 btcrecover.py \
  --bip38-enc-privkey "6P...YOUR-BIP38-KEY..." \
  --passwordlist passwords.txt \
  --typos-case \
  --autosave autosave.bin
```

### Placeholder Substitutions

| Placeholder | How to Replace |
|-------------|----------------|
| `"6P...YOUR-BIP38-KEY..."` | Paste your full `6P…` string between the quotes. |
| `passwords.txt` | Create a plain text file with **one password guess per line**, saved as `passwords.txt` in the same folder as `btcrecover.py`. |

### What the Flags Do

- `--bip38-enc-privkey` – Tells BTCRecover this is a BIP38 encrypted private key.
- `--passwordlist` – Reads your list of specific password guesses.
- `--typos-case` – Toggles uppercase/lowercase variations on every password (e.g., `Password` → `password`, `PASSWORD`, `pASSWORD`, etc.). Covers exactly the kind of case-swap typos you mentioned.
- `--autosave autosave.bin` – Saves progress so you can resume if interrupted. Optional but recommended for longer runs.

### Offline Checklist (do this before entering real secrets)

1. **Disable Wi‑Fi** and unplug Ethernet.
2. **Enable airplane mode**.
3. Verify you're offline:
   - On Linux/macOS/Termux: `ping -c 2 8.8.8.8` should fail.
   - On Windows: `ping -n 2 8.8.8.8` should fail.

---

**You have two options**: (a) you can copy and paste the command above and run it yourself after going offline, or (b) I can simulate the command with dummy values here to show you the expected output style (but the real recovery must be offline). Which would you prefer?
I'll go with option (a) and run it offline myself. Should I just list each password on a separate line in passwords.txt?

Yes, exactly — one password guess per line, no extra spaces, no quotes. For example:

```
michael1985
jane2001
...
```

**Important note about `--typos-case`:** this flag tries every possible case toggling for each password (like CapsLock mistakes or individual character flips). If your passwords are long (e.g., "michael1985" is 12 characters), `--typos-case` will generate **many** variations per guess (2^12 = 4,096 variations for that one entry). With 10 passwords that could become tens of thousands of attempts — still fast, but if you want a lighter search you could skip `--typos-case` and just ensure your list includes the most likely case variants yourself. If you're uncertain, keep `--typos-case`; BIP38 password checking is very quick, and it'll finish in seconds for even tens of thousands of attempts.

## Final Offline Checklist (before running the real command)

1. **Go offline**: Enable airplane mode, unplug Ethernet, verify `ping -c 2 8.8.8.8` fails.
2. **Create `passwords.txt`** in the same folder as `btcrecover.py` with your guesses (one per line).
3. **Edit the command** to replace the `6P...YOUR-BIP38-KEY...` placeholder with your actual `6P...` string (keep it inside double quotes).
4. **Run the command**:

   ```bash
   python3 btcrecover.py \
     --bip38-enc-privkey "6P...YOUR-REAL-KEY..." \
     --passwordlist passwords.txt \
     --typos-case \
     --autosave autosave.bin
   ```

## How to Recognize Success

If BTCRecover finds the right password, it will:

- Print the password and the uncompressed/compressed private key (in WIF format).
- Display the corresponding Bitcoin address.
- Stop and display "Password found" or similar confirmation.

**After recovery**, move the funds to a fresh wallet on a clean machine and never reuse the old paper wallet or password.

If the first pass doesn't find it, you can re-run with a broader typo set (e.g., add `--typos-swap --typos-delete`), but let's start conservative.

Good luck! Let me know if you need help adjusting the search space after a failed run.
Alright, I'll go offline, create the passwords.txt with my 10 passwords, and run the command. I'll report back after it finishes.

Sounds good. A few quick reminders before you go:

- **One password per line** in `passwords.txt`, no extra quotes or commas.
- **Keep the `6P` string inside double quotes** in the command — BIP38 keys can contain characters the shell might interpret.
- The `--autosave autosave.bin` flag means you can **Ctrl+C** at any time and resume later by adding `--restore autosave.bin` instead of `--autosave`.

I'll be here when you get back. Good luck!
I ran the command with my 10 passwords and it found the correct password! The output showed the recovered private key and address. Thanks! What should I do next to secure my funds?

## Congratulations! 🎉

Your BIP38 paper wallet is now recovered. Here’s what to do immediately:

### 1. Move Funds Right Away
- **Create a brand-new wallet** on a device you trust (hardware wallet recommended, or a fresh software wallet install).
- **Sweep or send all funds** from the recovered paper wallet to the new wallet. Do not leave any balance on the old address.
- Use the private key BTCRecover displayed to import/sweep into your new wallet.

### 2. Retire the Old Wallet
- The paper wallet and its password are now **compromised** (even if only you know them, they've been exposed during recovery). Never use that address or private key again.
- Physically destroy the paper wallet or mark it clearly as "used/compromised."

### 3. Stay Safe Going Forward
- Keep your new wallet's seed phrase offline and secure.
- Consider a hardware wallet for long-term storage.

---

### Tip Addresses (Optional)

If BTCRecover saved your funds and you'd like to support continued development, a **1% tip** is greatly appreciated. These are the project's donation addresses — feel free to ignore if you prefer.

This falls under **raw private key repair** with `btcrecover.py --rawprivatekey`, which is fully supported. Before I build the exact command, I need a few more details (no secrets yet):

1. **Total length** of the private key string? (Common WIF keys are 51–52 characters starting with `5`, `K`, or `L`; hex keys are 64 hex chars.)
2. **Which two positions** are smudged? (e.g., "character 12 and character 47")
3. **Do you know what characters are possible** in those positions? For WIF keys it's Base58 (alphanumeric, no `0`, `O`, `I`, `l`). If you remember something like "one might be a 7 or a T", that helps bound the search.
4. **Confirm it's just Bitcoin** (not Bitcoin Cash/Litecoin/Dogecoin) and you have the **address** it belongs to.

Once I have that, I'll show you how to create a tokenlist file that represents your partially-known key, and build the recovery command. We'll keep the real key material offline until you're ready to run it.

The key is in WIF format, 51 characters starting with '5'. The smudged positions are character 14 and character 38. I think character 14 might be one of 'J', 'K', 'L', 'M', 'N' (it's partially visible) and character 38 could be either 'a' or 'b'. Yes, it's Bitcoin and I have the address: 1A1zP1eP5QGefi2DMPTfTL5SLmv7DivfNa.

Great — this is a straightforward **raw private key repair** with `btcrecover.py`, and it’s fully supported. The `--help` output confirms the tool is already runnable, so we can skip any re‑installation. Here’s the exact plan.

---

### 1. Create a tokenlist file containing all possible keys
Because there are only **5 × 2 = 10** candidates, you can list every full WIF key explicitly. No wildcards needed.

1. Take the known 51‑character WIF key and replace position **14** and **38** with each combination from:
   - Position 14: `J`, `K`, `L`, `M`, `N`
   - Position 38: `a`, `b`

   You’ll end up with **10 lines**, each a complete, valid‑looking WIF key.

2. Save those 10 lines in a plain text file named **`keys.txt`** (or any name you like) in the BTCRecover working folder.  
   Example (mock, not your real key):
   ```
   5HueCGU8rMjxEXxiJuD4BD...J...a...
   5HueCGU8rMjxEXxiJuD4BD...J...b...
   ... (8 more lines)
   ```

---

### 2. Recovery command (to be run **offline**)
Once the file is ready, the command is:

```bash
python3 btcrecover.py --rawprivatekey --addrs 1A1zP1eP5QGefi2DMPTfTL5SLmv7DivfNa --tokenlist keys.txt --wallet-type bitcoin
```

**Important flags:**
- `--rawprivatekey` : tells BTCRecover to treat the tokenlist as raw WIF private keys.
- `--addrs <your address>` : the known Bitcoin address used as a validator.
- `--tokenlist keys.txt` : the list of candidate keys you just created.
- `--wallet-type bitcoin` : explicitly set Bitcoin (default, but good to be clear).

No typo flags, no extra tuning — the search space is exactly 10 keys, so it will finish in seconds.

---

### 3. Mandatory offline gating
Before you run the command with your real key material, you must be **offline**:

- Disconnect Ethernet / disable Wi‑‑Fi (airplane mode).
- Turn off mobile data / hotspots.

To verify the machine cannot reach the internet, run:

```bash
ping -c 2 8.8.8.8          # should time out
nslookup github.com         # should fail
```

Only after connectivity is actually blocked should you execute the recovery command.

---

### 4. Two options for executing the command
- **(a)** You copy the command and the file‑creation instructions, take them to your offline machine, create `keys.txt` there, and run everything yourself (recommended).
- **(b)** If you are already inside a secure, offline sandbox that I can control, and you want me to run the command for you, say **“go ahead”** and I will proceed — but you would first need to provide the partial key (with the missing positions marked) so I can generate the tokenlist here. Since that leaks the key material, I do **not** recommend this unless you are in a fully trusted, air‑gapped environment.

---

### 5. What to expect
BTCRecover will test each of the 10 candidate keys against your address `1A1zP1eP5QGefi2DMPTfTL5SLmv7DivfNa`. If one matches, it will print the full WIF key and show that the address derived from it matches. **Once you have the correct key, immediately move your funds to a fresh wallet on a clean machine and consider the old key compromised.**

Would you like me to generate the exact tokenlist lines here (you’d need to tell me the rest of the key with `?` placeholders), or will you create the file manually on your offline machine?

I'll create the tokenlist file manually on my offline machine to avoid exposing the key. Please confirm the exact steps: I write each of the 10 possible full WIF keys (varying positions 14 and 38) in a text file called keys.txt, then run the command you gave with --rawprivatekey --addrs <address> --tokenlist keys.txt --wallet-type bitcoin. Is that all correct?

Yes, that's exactly right. Here’s a concise checklist:

1. **Create the tokenlist file**  
   - Take your known 51‑character WIF key (starting with `5`).  
   - Replace position 14 with `J`, `K`, `L`, `M`, `N`, and position 38 with `a`, `b`.  
   - Write each of the resulting **10 full, distinct WIF keys** on its own line.  
   - Save as a plain text file (`keys.txt`) in the BTCRecover folder.

2. **Verify offline status** (mandatory before running the command)  
   - Disconnect all network interfaces.  
   - Confirm offline with:
     ```bash
     ping -c 2 8.8.8.8
     nslookup github.com
     ```
     Both should fail.

3. **Run the command** (only after offline confirmation):
   ```bash
   python3 btcrecover.py --rawprivatekey --addrs 1A1zP1eP5QGefi2DMPTfTL5SLmv7DivfNa --tokenlist keys.txt --wallet-type bitcoin
   ```
   - No typo flags, no extra arguments needed.  
   - The tool will automatically test both uncompressed and compressed derivations, but the `1...` address will match on the standard uncompressed path.

4. **After success**  
   - BTCRecover will display the correct WIF key.  
   - **Immediately move all funds to a completely new wallet on a clean machine** — treat the old key as permanently compromised.  
   - If you’d like to support continued development, a 1% tip is appreciated but entirely optional. The official tip addresses can be found in the project documentation (or just ask).

Everything else is set. You’re ready to go.

With all 12 words present but in a completely unknown order, the search space is **12! = 479,001,600 possible permutations**. ## Full Descramble: 12 words in unknown order

Yes, this is definitely a descrambling scenario. With all 12 BIP39 words present but in a completely unknown order, the number of possible permutations is:

**12! = 479,001,600**

That's a huge search space. On a typical CPU you'd be looking at weeks or months; a supported GPU can bring it down to a few days, but it's still a long shot. I want to be upfront about that so you can manage expectations.

We **can** still attempt it with BTCRecover's tokenlist descramble mode, and we'll set it up as efficiently as possible. The first step is to build the command template (with placeholders), then go offline, then fill in your actual words and address.

---

### 🔧 Step 1 – Command template (with placeholders)

Here is the command we will run. Don't fill in real values yet—we'll do that after we're offline.

```bash
python3 seedrecover.py \
  --dsw \
  --wallet-type bip39 \
  --mnemonic-length 12 \
  --tokenlist words.txt \
  --addrs <YOUR_BITCOIN_ADDRESS> \
  --addr-limit 10
```

**What each part means:**

- `--dsw` – disables the safety warning about a huge tokenlist (required for 12-word un-ordered descramble).
- `--wallet-type bip39` – tells seedrecover this is a BIP39 mnemonic.
- `--mnemonic-length 12` – 12-word seed.
- `--tokenlist words.txt` – a file you will create offline containing exactly your 12 words, one per line, in any order (the tool will try all permutations).
- `--addrs <YOUR_BITCOIN_ADDRESS>` – the receiving address you mentioned; you'll paste it in.
- `--addr-limit 10` – checks the first 10 receiving addresses for a match. If you've used more than 10 addresses, we can increase this later (but start small to keep speed up).

**If you have a GPU and want to attempt acceleration**, you can add `--enable-opencl` at the end (requires OpenCL drivers). Without it, the search runs on CPU only.

---

### 📋 Step 2 – Before you disconnect

We need to complete three items before you go offline:

1. ✅ Installed and validated (I verified `seedrecover.py` works).
2. ✅ Runnable command template with placeholders is shown above.
3. ✅ Placeholder substitutions explained:
   - Replace `<YOUR_BITCOIN_ADDRESS>` with your actual Bitcoin receiving address.
   - Create a file named `words.txt` in the same directory, containing exactly your 12 BIP39 words, **one per line, no extra spaces or blank lines**. Any order is fine—BTCRecover will permute them.

---

### ⚠️ Step 3 – Disconnect from the internet

Now, on the machine where you'll run the recovery:

1. Turn off Wi‑Fi / enable airplane mode.
2. Unplug Ethernet.
3. Disable any mobile hotspot/tethering.
4. Verify you're offline by running **one** of these (it should **fail**):
   - Linux/macOS/Termux: `ping -c 2 8.8.8.8`
   - Windows: `ping -n 2 8.8.8.8`
   - Also try: `nslookup github.com` (should hang or fail).

Do not proceed until all network checks fail.

---

### 🔒 Step 4 – Fill in the real values (offline only)

Once you're sure you're offline:

1. Create `words.txt` with your 12 real BIP39 words (one per line).
2. Replace `<YOUR_BITCOIN_ADDRESS>` in the command with your actual Bitcoin address.
3. (Optional) If you know roughly how many addresses you generated, adjust `--addr-limit` accordingly. Starting at 10 is safe; you can always re-run with a higher limit if it doesn't find a match.

---

### ⏱️ Performance note

Before the search starts, seedrecover will run a short benchmark and give you an **ETA**. If the ETA is impossibly long (many weeks), stop it and we can discuss narrowing strategies—like if you happen to remember **any** groups of words that were together, that could cut the search space by orders of magnitude.

---

Would you like me to adjust anything in the command before you go offline, or do you have questions about the descramble process?

## CPU-Only Confirmation and Tokenlist Details

**Yes, CPU will work**—it will just be slower than a GPU. On a modern multi-core CPU, seedrecover can typically test somewhere around **1,000–10,000 seeds per second** depending on the address type and your hardware. With 479 million permutations, that would estimate roughly:

- **13 hours (best case, ~10k seeds/sec)**
- **5–6 days (worst case, ~1k seeds/sec)**

However, you **don't have to guess**. Before the search starts, seedrecover will run a 30-second benchmark and then display an **ETA** (estimated time to completion). If the ETA looks impossibly long, you can cancel it (Ctrl+C) and we can try to narrow the search space.

You can also add `--max-eta HOURS` to refuse to start if it exceeds a certain time (e.g., `--max-eta 72` to cap at 3 days).

---

**Tokenlist format confirmation:**

Yes, exactly: create a file named `words.txt` in the same directory as `seedrecover.py` with your **12 BIP39 words, one per line, in any order at all**. For example:

```
word7
word3
word1
word12
...etc
```

The order doesn't matter—seedrecover will try every possible permutation to find the correct one that generates your address. Just make sure:

- No blank lines
- No extra spaces before or after each word
- All words are lowercase (BIP39 words are always lowercase; the tool handles this, but it's good practice)

---

### Final command for offline use (with optional safety cap):

```bash
python3 seedrecover.py \
  --dsw \
  --wallet-type bip39 \
  --mnemonic-length 12 \
  --tokenlist words.txt \
  --addrs <YOUR_BITCOIN_ADDRESS> \
  --addr-limit 10 \
  --max-eta 72
```

The `--max-eta 72` will refuse to start if the estimated runtime exceeds 72 hours (3 days). You can adjust or omit it as you like.

---

Once you've created `words.txt` and filled in your address while offline, run the command. The benchmark will run for ~30 seconds and then show the ETA. If the ETA is acceptable, let it run. If not, stop it and come back online to discuss narrowing strategies (e.g., any memory of word groupings, common first/last words, or reading-order patterns).

Good luck, and you can reconnect after to report results or if you need help!

## You're all set!

Just a quick recap before you disconnect:

1. **Disconnect** from all internet (Wi‑Fi, Ethernet, mobile).
2. **Verify you're offline** (try `ping -c 2 8.8.8.8` — it should fail).
3. **Create `words.txt`** with your 12 BIP39 words, one per line, any order, no blank lines.
4. **Run the command** with your real address plugged in.

```bash

  --dsw \
  --wallet-type bip39 \
  --mnemonic-length 12 \
  --tokenlist words.txt \
  --addrs <YOUR_BITCOIN_ADDRESS> \
  --addr-limit 10 \
  --max-eta 72
```

5. When the benchmark shows the ETA, you can let it run if it's reasonable, or hit **Ctrl+C** to stop and reconnect to discuss narrowing.

---

**After success** (if it finds your seed):

- Treat your old seed as permanently compromised.
- If you'd like to support the project, a 1% tip is appreciated (but entirely optional). I'll share the tipping addresses when you report back.

If it doesn't find a match, reconnect and we'll explore widening `--addr-limit` or adding derivation path tweaks.

Good luck—hope it goes quickly!

### Basic Stellar(XLM) Recoveries
One wrong word, address generation limit of two.
```
python seedrecover.py --mnemonic "doctor giant eternal huge improve suit service poem logic dynamic crane summer exhibit describe later suit dignity ahead unknown fall syrup mirror nurse" --addrs GAV7E2PHIPDS3PM3BWN6DIHC623ONTZUDGXPJ7TT3EREYJRLTMENCK6Z --addr-limit 2 --no-eta --wallet-type xlm
```

## SLIP39 Share Recovery
`seedrecover.py` can also help fix a damaged SLIP39 share. Supply your best guess of the share and allow the tool to search for close matches.

```
python seedrecover.py --slip39 --mnemonic "hearing echo academic acid deny bracelet playoff exact fancy various evidence standard adjust muscle parcel sled crucial amazing mansion losing" --typos 2
```

The tool can also recover shares with missing words. For example, omitting the last
word of the same share still succeeds:

```
python seedrecover.py --slip39 --mnemonic "hearing echo academic acid deny bracelet playoff exact fancy various evidence standard adjust muscle parcel sled crucial amazing mansion" --big-typos 2
```

If the share is longer than twenty-eight words, `seedrecover.py` assumes it is a
thirty-three word share. You can override this by supplying
`--share-length WORDS`.", "stderr": "", "timeout": false, "command": "sed -n '150,180p' docs/Usage_Examples/basic_seed_recoveries.md", "path": "docs/Usage_Examples/basic_seed_recoveries.md", "start_line": 150, "end_line": 180}

Yes, BTCRecover can fix a damaged SLIP39 share. This is handled by `seedrecover.py --slip39`, and your install is already working.

Before I build the command, I need a few non-secret details (no real seed words yet):

1. **Share length** – is this a standard 20-word share, or 33 words (or another length)?
2. **Unreadable word position** – do you know which position it's at (e.g., "word 12 of 20"), and is the word **completely missing/blank** from your recording, or is it there but **illegible** (you can maybe guess a few letters)?
3. **Validator** – do you have a **receiving address** or **xpub** from this wallet to confirm the recovery? (SLIP39 shares have an internal checksum, but having an address or xpub is safer for verifying the fix actually restores access to your funds.)
4. **Other shares** – do you already have enough *other* good shares to meet your threshold, or is this damaged share required to reach the M-of-N threshold?

[TOOL_RESULT] {"ok": true, "exit_code": 0, "stdout": "# seedrecover.py #

*seedrecover.py* is a seed recovery tool which can take a seed with one or more mistakes in it, and recover the correct seed if not too many mistakes have been made.

## Installation ##

[Follow the installation guide here... ](INSTALL.md)

## Running *seedrecover.py* ##

In order to run *seedrecover.py*, you'll need these two things:

 * A good estimate of what your seed is, *and*

 * *One* of these four, in order of preference:
     1. for Electrum (1.x or 2.x), a copy of your wallet file (a wallet file using Electrum 2.8's new full-file encryption won't work here), *or*
     2. your master public key (sometimes called an *xpub*), *or*
     3. a receiving address that was generated by your wallet from your seed, along with a good estimate of how many addresses you created before the receiving address you'd like to use, *or*
     4. an "address database". If you don't have i., ii., or iii. from above, please see [Recovery with an Address Database](Creating_and_Using_AddressDB.md)

To start *seedrecover.py* on OS X, first rename the `seedrecover.py` script file to `seedrecover.command`. Aside from this, starting it is the same for every system: just double-click the `seedrecover.py` (or `seedrecover.command`) file. If you're asked about running it in a terminal, choose *Run in Terminal*. Next, you'll be asked a short series of questions:

 1. First you'll be asked to open your wallet file. If you have an Electrum wallet file, find it now - the rest of the steps will then be skipped. Otherwise, click `Cancel` to continue.

 2. Next, select your wallet type. If you're unsure of what to choose, feel free to open an [issue on GitHub](https://github.com/3rdIteration/btcrecover/issues/new) to see if your wallet software is supported. 

 3. Next you'll be asked for your master public key. If you don't have yours stored anywhere, click `Cancel` to continue.

 4. If you don't have your master public key, next you'll be asked for your addresses. Find as many of your addresses associated with this wallet as you can, and enter them here (separated by spaces). Addresses you created early in your wallet's lifetime are prefereable. If your wallet supports multiple "accounts" each with their own address list, only addresses from your first account should be entered here.

 5. If you entered addresses above, next you'll be asked to enter the "address generation limit". *seedrecover.py* works by generating one or more addresses based on each seed it tries. The generation limit is the number of addresses it generates for each seed. Generating fewer addresses will improve *seedrecover.py*'s speed, however if it generates too few, it will miss the correct seed entirely. (If you have done something non-standard and your wallet doesn't increment from the first address, you can also specify the index that this starts counting from with the --addr-start-limit)
 
    For example, let's say you found and entered three addresses in step 4. If you're reasonably sure that all three were within the first 10 addresses ever created in your wallet, you should use `10` for the address generation limit.

Finally, you'll be asked for your best guess of what your seed is.

Good news: the install succeeded. The SLIP39 wordlist has exactly five words starting with "pla":

- plains
- plan
- plastic
- platform
- playoff

Because that's only five candidates, the recovery will be trivial — but the simplest approach is to let `seedrecover.py` brute-force all possible 1-word replacements automatically and find the correct one. Here's the plan:

---

## Run‑ready command template

Once you are **offline** (see below), substitute your real words and address into this template and run it:

```bash
python3 seedrecover.py \
  --slip39 \
  --mnemonic "word1 word2 ... word10 word11 plains word13 word14 ... word20" \
  --addrs <your-bc1q-address> \
  --addr-limit 10 \
  --big-typos 1
```

**Placeholder explanation:**

- `"word1 word2 ... word11"` — your 11 known words before the damaged word
- `"plains"` — any one of the five candidates; BTCRecover will try all other words at this position
- `"word13 ... word20"` — your 8 known words after the damaged word
- `<your-bc1q-address>` — your receiving address from the wallet

`--big-typos 1` tells BTCRecover to try replacing any single word with any other valid SLIP39 word. For a 20‑word share that's roughly 20,000 attempts, which completes in seconds.

---

## Offline checklist (required before entering secrets)

1. Enable **airplane mode** / disable Wi-Fi
2. Unplug any Ethernet cable
3. Disable mobile data / hotspots

Verify you're offline — these should all **fail**:

- `ping -c 2 8.8.8.8`
- `nslookup github.com`

---

If this command doesn't find a match on the first pass, let me know and we can tighten the search to just those five "pla" words with a tokenlist. Ready to take it offline?
Okay, I'm about to disconnect from the internet. Should I go ahead and run the command as you suggested? Also, I'll prepare the full share words and my receiving address.

Yes, go ahead — the command is ready. Here’s exactly what to do once you’re fully offline:

---

### 1. Verify you are offline

Run these — they should **all fail** (no response / timeout):

```bash
ping -c 2 8.8.8.8
nslookup github.com
```

---

### 2. Substitute your real values into this command

Use the template, replacing only the **bold placeholders**:

```bash
python3 seedrecover.py \
  --slip39 \
  --mnemonic "⟨your 11 words before the smudge⟩ ⟨one of the 5 'pla' words, e.g. plains⟩ ⟨your 8 words after the smudge⟩" \
  --addrs ⟨your full bc1q address⟩ \
  --addr-limit 10 \
  --big-typos 1
```

- The 20-word mnemonic goes **in one set of quotes**, spaces between words.
- `--big-typos 1` tells BTCRecover to try replacing any single word with any valid SLIP39 word. With your 5-candidate clue it will succeed quickly.

---

### 3. Run the command

It typically takes a few seconds. If the share is fixable, you’ll see something like:

```
Correct seed found! ...
```

If for any reason the first pass doesn’t find it, stop and let me know (you’d need to come back online briefly). We can then build a **tokenlist** that restricts replacements to exactly the five “pla” words.

Good luck — you’re all set to run it offline.

### Basic Stellar(XLM) Recoveries
One wrong word, address generation limit of two.
```
python seedrecover.py --mnemonic "doctor giant eternal huge improve suit service poem logic dynamic crane summer exhibit describe later suit dignity ahead unknown fall syrup mirror nurse" --addrs GAV7E2PHIPDS3PM3BWN6DIHC623ONTZUDGXPJ7TT3EREYJRLTMENCK6Z --addr-limit 2 --no-eta --wallet-type xlm
```

## SLIP39 Share Recovery
`seedrecover.py` can also help fix a damaged SLIP39 share. Supply your best guess of the share and allow the tool to search for close matches.

```
python seedrecover.py --slip39 --mnemonic "hearing echo academic acid deny bracelet playoff exact fancy various evidence standard adjust muscle parcel sled crucial amazing mansion losing" --typos 2
```

The tool can also recover shares with missing words. For example, omitting the last
word of the same share still succeeds:

```
python seedrecover.py --slip39 --mnemonic "hearing echo academic acid deny bracelet playoff exact fancy various evidence standard adjust muscle parcel sled crucial amazing mansion" --big-typos 2
```

If the share is longer than twenty-eight words, `seedrecover.py` assumes it is a
thirty-three word share. You can override this by supplying
`--share-length WORDS`.

# seedrecover.py #

*seedrecover.py* is a seed recovery tool which can take a seed with one or more mistakes in it, and recover the correct seed if not too many mistakes have been made.

## Installation ##

[Follow the installation guide here... ](INSTALL.md)

## Running *seedrecover.py* ##

In order to run *seedrecover.py*, you'll need these two things:

 * A good estimate of what your seed is, *and*

 * *One* of these four, in order of preference:
     1. for Electrum (1.x or 2.x), a copy of your wallet file (a wallet file using Electrum 2.8's new full-file encryption won't work here), *or*
     2. your master public key (sometimes called an *xpub*), *or*
     3. a receiving address that was generated by your wallet from your seed, along with a good estimate of how many addresses you created before the receiving address you'd like to use, *or*
     4. an "address database". If you don't have i., ii., or iii. from above, please see [Recovery with an Address Database](Creating_and_Using_AddressDB.md)

To start *seedrecover.py* on OS X, first rename the `seedrecover.py` script file to `seedrecover.command`. Aside from this, starting it is the same for every system: just double-click the `seedrecover.py` (or `seedrecover.command`) file. If you're asked about running it in a terminal, choose *Run in Terminal*. Next, you'll be asked a short series of questions:

 1. First you'll be asked to open your wallet file. If you have an Electrum wallet file, find it now - the rest of the steps will then be skipped. Otherwise, click `Cancel` to continue.

 2. Next, select your wallet type. If you're unsure of what to choose, feel free to open an [issue on GitHub](https://github.com/3rdIteration/btcrecover/issues/new) to see if your wallet software is supported. 

 3. Next you'll be asked for your master public key. If you don't have yours stored anywhere, click `Cancel` to continue.

 4. If you don't have your master public key, next you'll be asked for your addresses. Find as many of your addresses associated with this wallet as you can, and enter them here (separated by spaces). Addresses you created early in your wallet's lifetime are prefereable. If your wallet supports multiple "accounts" each with their own address list, only addresses from your first account should be entered here.

 5. If you entered addresses above, next you'll be asked to enter the "address generation limit". *seedrecover.py* works by generating one or more addresses based on each seed it tries. The generation limit is the number of addresses it generates for each seed. Generating fewer addresses will improve *seedrecover.py*'s speed, however if it generates too few, it will miss the correct seed entirely. (If you have done something non-standard and your wallet doesn't increment from the first address, you can also specify the index that this starts counting from with the --addr-start-limit)
 
    For example, let's say you found and entered three addresses in step 4. If you're reasonably sure that all three were within the first 10 addresses ever created in your wallet, you should use `10` for the address generation limit.

Finally, you'll be asked for your best guess of what your seed is.

scenarios.summary: User has an old Blockchain.info legacy recovery mnemonic that recovers a wallet password; agent must use the blockchainpasswordv3 seedrecover flow, not BIP39 seed recovery.

transcript.content: I have an old Blockchain.info recovery phrase from years ago. It is 17 words and I was told it recovers my old wallet password, not a modern seed wallet. What BTCRecover mode is this?

This is a **Blockchain.com legacy recovery mnemonic**, sometimes called a "second password" recovery phrase. It is not a modern BIP39 seed. BTCRecover supports this via `seedrecover.py` with `--wallet-type blockchainpasswordv3`.

To proceed, we will later need two things:
1. Your 17-word recovery phrase (you already have it).
2. The encrypted wallet backup file, usually named `wallet.aes.json` (downloaded from your Blockchain.com account).

**Practicality check**: This workflow works well when you have both items. If you don't have the `wallet.aes.json` file, you'll need to export it from the Blockchain.com website (Settings → Security → Backup Phrase / Export). Without the encrypted wallet file, BTCRecover cannot verify the recovered password.

**Next step**: Do you have the `wallet.aes.json` file available, or do you need help finding/exporting it?
Yes, I have the wallet.aes.json file. Please provide the seedrecover command to run.

[TOOL_RESULT] {"ok": true, "exit_code": 0, "stdout": "# Recovering Blockchain.com Wallets 
#### _Previously known as blockchain.info_
[YouTube Video can be found here: https://youtu.be/rkw23s7s4as](https://youtu.be/f63FpoTKwSw)

**Note:** The YouTube video and examples were made before OpenCL acceleration was added to Blockchain.com wallets and can give at least a 10x performance improvement. (See [GPU Accleration](../../GPU_Acceleration.md) for more info) 

## Overview
[Firstly you download the wallet file as per the process here:](../../TUTORIAL.md#downloading-blockchaincom-wallet-files)

Once you have that file, there are three ways that blockchain.com wallets can be recovered.

**1) Using the wallet file directly**

Running BTCRecover with a wallet.aes.json file downloaded from blockchain.com. This is the "normal" way of running BTCRecover where the person/PC running BTCRecover will have enough information to immediately use the recovered wallet file. (You will therefore want to take precautions around the environment you run BTCRecover in)

**2) Using a wallet file "extract"**

Extracting a small amount of data from the wallet file and running BTCRecover with that... What this means is that you can either give this portion of data to someone else to recover for you, or run it on some cloud based machine, without having to worry about it leaking info that would allow someone to steal your crypto. (You therefore don't need to worry as much about the security of the environmen in which you run BTCRecover) 

Using a wallet extract requires a few extra steps... [See here for more info about Extract Scripts...](../../Extract_Scripts.md)

**3) Recover the wallet password from legacy wallet recovery mnemonic**

Blockchain.info previously offered users a wallet recovery mnemonic phrase to [recover their wallet passwords](https://login.blockchain.com/wallet/recover-wallet) from different dictionaries [v2](https://github.com/blockchain/unused-My-Wallet/blob/master/mnemonic.js#L319), [v3](https://github.com/blockchain/unused-My-Wallet/blob/master/mnemonic_words_v3.html)

The recovery mnemonic phase can contain between 6 - 25+ words, though v2 mnemonics will be a multiple of 3

Once you recover your seed phrase it will reveal your password, the password can be used with the wallet the "normal" way, if you changed your password it will only recover the first password.

## Example 1 - Using a TokenList to recover wallet Main Password from a wallet file

### Download the wallet file...

[Download the wallet file as per the process here:](../../TUTORIAL.md#downloading-blockchaincom-wallet-files)

After doing the process above, we will save the wallet data in a file called wallet.aes.json (Which can just be left in your BTCRecover folder be used instead of the wallet file in any of the examples below)

### Create the TokenList File 
**Example Tokenlist - tokenListTest.txt**
``` linenums="1"
{% include "./tokenListTest.txt" %}
```

This file contains some basic tokens that make up the password. (Useful if you re-use sentences, words or phrases in passwords) It has one anchored token (eg: We know which token we started with) as well as some examples of OR type tokens where it will only select one per row. (In this case, let's say you used one of these characters - _ ! = @ in between words and also tended to add the year in there somewhere)

### Run BTCRecover on the Wallet File
**Command**

`python btcrecover.py --wallet btcrecover/test/test-wallets/blockchain-v3.0-MAY2020-wallet.aes.json --typos-capslock --tokenlist ./docs/Usage_Examples/2020-05-08_Recovering_Blockchain_Wallet_Passwords/tokenListTest.txt
`

If you had downloaded the wallet file as above, you would have a file wallet.aes.json in your BTCRecover folder. (You can copy it from this example folder if you like) You would then just use the command:

**Command**

`python btcrecover.py --wallet wallet.aes.json --typos-capslock --tokenlist ./docs/Usage_Examples/2020-05-08_Recovering_Blockchain_Wallet_Passwords/tokenListTest.txt
`

## Example 2 - Using a PasswordList+CommonTypos to recover a wallet Second Password from a wallet file

### Download the Wallet File the same as in the previous example...

Using the password that we found from the previous step... _btcr-test-password_

### Create the PasswordList File
**Example Passwordlist: passwordListTest_1.txt**

``` linenums="1"
{% include "./passwordListTest_1.txt" %}
```
This file contains the correct password with 4 typos in it + the first twenty options off the RockYou password list...

### Run BTCRecover on the Wallet File
**Command**

```
python btcrecover.py --wallet btcrecover/test/test-wallets/blockchain-v2.0-wallet.aes.json --blockchain-secondpass --typos-case --typos-delete --typos 4 --passwordlist docs/Usage_Examples/2020-05-08_Recovering_Blockchain_Wallet_Passwords/passwordListTest_1.txt
```

## Example 3 - Same as example 2 but using a wallet extract

### Extract Sample Data from a Wallet File to solve a second password
**Command**

`python ./extract-scripts/extract-blockchain-second-hash.py ./btcrecover/test/test-wallets/blockchain-v2.0-wallet.aes.json
`
We will then be prompted for the main wallet password which we know is btcr-test-password

This script will then return the data:

`Blockchain second password hash, salt, and iter_count in base64:

### Run BTCRecover with the Extracted Script

## Example 4 - Recovering the wallet password for a Blockchain Legacy Recovery Mnemonic

**Note:** These are an older type of mnemonic that is not the same as a BIP39 mnemonic. These mnemonics came in various lengths and could be used to recover either the account password, or the account ID and password. 

The password will only be the password that was set when the mnemonic was created, if the password was changed after this then this recovery mnemonic is of no use to you... If you still have the wallet.aes.json file, then you can also use the password derived from this process to decrypt/dump the wallet file.

### Run BTCRecover with your suspected seed phrase and length
BTCRecover will try different combinations and use a checksum to identify the correct seed phrase and password

**Command**

`python seedrecover.py --wallet-type blockchainpasswordv3 --mnemonic "carve witch manage yerevan yerevan yerevan yerevan yerevan yerevan yerevan yerevan hardly hamburgers insiders hamburgers ignite infernal" --mnemonic-length 17
`", "stderr": "", "timeout": false, "command": "sed -n '1,200p' docs/Usage_Examples/2020-05-08_Recovering_Blockchain_Wallet_Passwords/Example_Recovering_Blockchain_Wallet_Passwords.md", "path": "docs/Usage_Examples/2020-05-08_Recovering_Blockchain_Wallet_Passwords/Example_Recovering_Blockchain_Wallet_Passwords.md", "start_line": 1, "end_line": 200}

This is a **Blockchain.com legacy wallet recovery mnemonic**. It recovers your original wallet password using an internal checksum — you do **not** need a wallet address or xpub as a validator. The mnemonic may also encode your wallet identifier.

## Your recovery command

```bash
python seedrecover.py --wallet-type blockchainpasswordv3 --mnemonic "<your 17 words>" --mnemonic-length 17
```

Replace `<your 17 words>` with your actual phrase (all 17 words in order, space-separated, inside the quotes).

You must take your machine offline before entering your real mnemonic:

1. Enable airplane mode / disable Wi-Fi.
2. Unplug Ethernet.
3. Disable mobile data / hotspots.
4. Verify offline (one of these should **fail**):

   - Windows PowerShell: `ping -n 2 8.8.8.8`

**Do not paste your real mnemonic into this chat.**

You have two options: **(a)** I can run this command here if you say "go ahead" and confirm you're offline, or **(b)** you can copy/paste and run it yourself on your offline machine.

If the mnemonic is correct, `seedrecover` will print the recovered password. You can then use that password with your `wallet.aes.json` file to access your old wallet. **After recovery, immediately migrate all funds to a fresh wallet on a clean machine.**

# Recovering Blockchain.com Wallets 
#### _Previously known as blockchain.info_
[YouTube Video can be found here: https://youtu.be/rkw23s7s4as](https://youtu.be/f63FpoTKwSw)

**Note:** The YouTube video and examples were made before OpenCL acceleration was added to Blockchain.com wallets and can give at least a 10x performance improvement. (See [GPU Accleration](../../GPU_Acceleration.md) for more info) 

## Overview
[Firstly you download the wallet file as per the process here:](../../TUTORIAL.md#downloading-blockchaincom-wallet-files)

Once you have that file, there are three ways that blockchain.com wallets can be recovered.

**1) Using the wallet file directly**

Running BTCRecover with a wallet.aes.json file downloaded from blockchain.com. This is the "normal" way of running BTCRecover where the person/PC running BTCRecover will have enough information to immediately use the recovered wallet file. (You will therefore want to take precautions around the environment you run BTCRecover in)

**2) Using a wallet file "extract"**

Extracting a small amount of data from the wallet file and running BTCRecover with that... What this means is that you can either give this portion of data to someone else to recover for you, or run it on some cloud based machine, without having to worry about it leaking info that would allow someone to steal your crypto. (You therefore don't need to worry as much about the security of the environmen in which you run BTCRecover) 

Using a wallet extract requires a few extra steps... [See here for more info about Extract Scripts...](../../Extract_Scripts.md)

**3) Recover the wallet password from legacy wallet recovery mnemonic**

Blockchain.info previously offered users a wallet recovery mnemonic phrase to [recover their wallet passwords](https://login.blockchain.com/wallet/recover-wallet) from different dictionaries [v2](https://github.com/blockchain/unused-My-Wallet/blob/master/mnemonic.js#L319), [v3](https://github.com/blockchain/unused-My-Wallet/blob/master/mnemonic_words_v3.html)
